brief_document.title: Package scaffold, public root, and the @external binding layer
brief_document.purpose: Stand up the aion_flow Gleam package and the single internal module that owns every @external(erlang) binding to the engine NIFs. This is the foundation every other brief builds on: a pure-Gleam, Hex-publishable package with one auditable binding seam.
brief_document.task: Create the gleam/aion_flow package with a gleam.toml declaring gleam_stdlib and gleam_json as the only runtime dependencies. Create the top-level aion_flow.gleam package-doc / re-export module. Create aion/internal/ffi.gleam holding every @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", "<fn>") declaration the SDK needs, each with the type-erased string/Result signature the engine NIFs expose. No other module in the package declares an @external. The package must build, check, and format clean with no engine present.

requirements.title: Package scaffold
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL create a valid Gleam package at gleam/aion_flow whose gleam.toml declares the package name, version, and Hex metadata with gleam_stdlib and gleam_json as the only runtime dependencies.
acceptance: gleam/aion_flow/gleam.toml declares name = "aion_flow", a version, a description, and a licences/repository field set for Hex publication.
Dependencies are exactly gleam_stdlib and gleam_json (plus dev-dependency gleeunit for tests); no Rust, beamr, or engine dependency appears.
gleam build, gleam check, and gleam format --check pass clean for the package.
The package has no Rust source and no build-script dependency on the engine crates.

requirements.title: The @external binding layer
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define aion/internal/ffi as the single module declaring every @external(erlang, "aion_flow_ffi", "<fn>") binding to the engine NIFs. No other module SHALL declare an @external.
acceptance: aion/internal/ffi.gleam declares the @external bindings the SDK needs (activity dispatch, now, random, sleep/start_timer/cancel_timer, signal receive/send, query register/reply, child spawn/await, concurrency collectors), each using the Erlang module name "aion_flow_ffi".
Each binding's Gleam signature uses the type-erased ffi form (String payloads, Result(String, String) returns) — the typed wrappers live in the author-facing modules.

A doc comment states that "aion_flow_ffi" is the engine's NIF registry namespace registered via EngineBuilder::register_nifs (AE-004), resolved by beamr at runtime, and that gleam build type-checks the signatures with no engine present.
No author-facing module anywhere in the package declares an @external (verified by inspection / a grep-style check in review).

requirements.title: Public root module
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide aion_flow.gleam as the top-level package-doc module that re-exports the curated public surface and contains no @external and no business logic.
acceptance: aion_flow.gleam carries a module doc comment describing the package and the recorded/deterministic discipline at a high level.
It re-exports (or documents the import paths for) the author-facing modules; it contains no @external declaration and no logic beyond re-exports.
gleam build passes with the root module present.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement any engine NIF — the native side is AE/AN. This brief declares the Gleam @external signatures only.
SHALL NOT define the typed author-facing wrappers (activity, workflow, signal, etc.) — those are later briefs; this brief only declares the raw ffi bindings they will call.
SHALL NOT add a Rust crate or beamr dependency to the package.
verification: No todo, panic, or placeholder is left in any module.

checklist.text: gleam/aion_flow is a valid Gleam package: gleam.toml declares the package name, version, and Hex metadata, with gleam_stdlib and gleam_json as the only runtime dependencies.
gleam build, gleam check, and gleam format --check all pass clean for the package with no engine present.
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi; no author-facing module declares an @external.
All ffi bindings use the Erlang module name "aion_flow_ffi" (the engine's NIF registry namespace registered via AE-004).
aion_flow.gleam is a top-level package-doc module re-exporting the curated public surface; it contains no @external and no business logic.

stories.text: As the engine, I want every SDK primitive to bind to a NIF in one known registry namespace (aion_flow_ffi) so that I can register the native implementations in one place and have compiled workflows resolve against them.
As the package maintainer, I want aion_flow to be pure Gleam with no Rust or engine dependency so that it publishes to Hex standalone and type-checks with no engine present.

constraints.text: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

resolved_context.intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
